narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as an uphill battle—83.8% chance of not passing, based on patterns from thousands of students like you. Picture your story through the model's eyes: it started with your Year 3 grades landing you in the bottom decile of your school, the single strongest signal pulling toward failure, as if whispering that the rigors of legal study were a tough match by the end. That echoed back to your Year 1 decile, also at the bottom, reinforcing a pattern of early struggles that the model couldn't shake. Your LSAT score of 27 weighed heavily too, another big nudge toward the risk side, suggesting the analytical demands of law exams might prove challenging. Layered on that was your middle-family income bracket, which the model links to added pressures without the safety nets higher incomes sometimes provide. Your background as a Black woman entered the picture as well, statistically tilting toward higher hurdles in this arena, though your gender actually nudged things slightly in a positive direction. Even your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.2 and full-time student status pulled toward failure in the model's view, painting a full portrait of someone facing compounded odds. It's not about your spirit or potential—we see your target drive for law school—but these combined threads wove a cautious tale the model couldn't ignore. This isn't the end of your story; many have rewritten predictions like yours through targeted prep. What if you revisited those early study habits or LSAT strategies? You've got resilience—use it to pivot and prove the odds wrong. We're rooting for your comeback.
